glossary.definition: The ability to learn, reason, solve problems, and adapt to new situations. The concept is studied in depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc., and is assessed using psychological tests. Intelligence can be a single ability or a set of abilities, and is influenced by linguistic, cultural, physical, and other differences.
glossary.sentence: Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. However, it is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence.

glossary.definition: deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020). It is a part of computer science, and it also consists of many sub-fields such as robotics, evolutionary computations, intelligent agents, knowledge representation, and others.

glossary.definition: An ideal intelligence as a standard, used in some categories of definitions of artificial intelligence. Rationality is based on logics, where knowledge is represented as logical rules.
glossary.sentence: The categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4). Each category of definitions of artificial intelligence is based on a specific approach used to develop intelligent computer systems (Russell and Norvig, 2009): A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers. Systems that think rationally are based on logics . As a result, knowledge is represented as logical rules, for example, if we know that 'All students have a student ID' and 'Alex is a student', than we can conclude (to infer new knowledge) that 'Alex has a student ID'.

glossary.definition: A test underlies systems that act humanly. It is a measure of whether a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior that is indistinguishable from that of a human.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: A specific approach used to develop intelligent computer systems, which considers human being to be an information processing system. It focuses on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.

glossary.definition: A form of intelligent performance based on logics, where knowledge is represented as logical rules. Rationality is a standard used in some categories of definitions of artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics . As a result, knowledge is represented as logical rules, for example, if we know that 'All students have a student ID' and 'Alex is a student', than we can conclude (to infer new knowledge) that 'Alex has a student ID'.

glossary.definition: The ability to learn, reason, solve problems, and adapt to new situations, as studied in depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. It is influenced by linguistic, cultural, physical, and other differences.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, this field of science has also developed a set of tests allowing assessment of the level of human intellectual development.

glossary.definition: The ability to learn, reason, solve problems, and adapt to new situations, as related to the learning ability of animals such as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. It is much more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence, because animal diversity is greater and their cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and the ways how they explore the environment in which they live are more complex.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence . It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence, because animal diversity is even greater taking into account their cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and the ways how they explore the environment in which they live.

glossary.definition: The ability to learn, reason, solve problems, and adapt to new situations, as related to the intellectual performance of plants. Plants have many forms of intellectual performance, such as learning based on repeated practice, memory, perception of shape, colour, smell and sound, and a 'sense of place' and an awareness of the neighbourhood. Plants do not have a brain and nervous system.
glossary.sentence: At the same time, plants possess many forms of intellectual performance, such as (Gagliano, 2015) : they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a 'sense of place' and an awareness of the neighbourhood. 'A plant continually gathers and updates diverse information about its environment, integrates this with information on its present internal state, and then makes decisions that reconcile its well-being with its environment' (Trewavas, 2016)

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems
glossary.sentence: Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable

glossary.definition: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable; Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable
glossary.sentence: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable; Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable

glossary.definition: that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007)

glossary.definition: or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015)
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015)

glossary.definition: It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales
glossary.sentence: It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales

glossary.definition: a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food
glossary.sentence: ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food

glossary.definition: studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish
glossary.sentence: particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: Methods that do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Methods based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration. Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence. Thus not all intelligent systems need the knowledge base because intelligent computer systems can also be developed using weak methods (symbolic manipulation or mathematical calculations).

glossary.definition: a computer system that uses heuristics and game theory algorithms to make decisions and select moves in a game, based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning.
glossary.sentence: The current node is assigned the heuristic measure indicating the best state that can be reached after performing three steps. Based on this heuristic measure, the intelligent computer system selects moves that lead to the identified best state.

glossary.definition: A representation of the problem as a graph where nodes represent states and edges represent transitions between states.
glossary.sentence: We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, for example, f(n) = the number of tiles out of place f(n) = the sum of distances f(n) = the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: A process where a solution to a problem is found by solving a problem.
glossary.sentence: Playing a game can be seen as a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A form of activity in which players engage with each other or in competition, using agreed upon rules and sometimes for a pre-determined purpose.
glossary.sentence: Let's take the game of tic-tac-toe as an example.

glossary.definition: a game where the system has a state space graph of the game, and the system generates descendants of the initial state of the game and applies the defined heuristic evaluation function.
glossary.sentence: Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe. The system has a state space graph of the game. In this demonstration we will consider only the initial state of the game and its three descendants (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: A point or element in a graph, representing a state of the game.
glossary.sentence: Its nodes correspond to discrete states of the game.

glossary.definition: A line connecting two nodes in a graph, representing a legal move in the game.
glossary.sentence: Arcs are used to represent legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: A level in the state space graph, representing states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.
glossary.sentence: Node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A legal move made by a player in the game.
glossary.sentence: Each of the possible moves produces a specific intermediate state.

glossary.definition: A characteristic or property of the states in the game, used to represent the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time.

glossary.definition: The total number of marks a player has placed in the game grid.
glossary.sentence: The alternate score of the players is 8:8.

glossary.definition: A sequence of numbers used to represent the state of the game.
glossary.sentence: A numerical string “2-2-2-3-3” is given at the beginning of the game.

glossary.definition: A node in the state space graph that has no outgoing arcs, representing a final state of the game.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph of the game also represents a data structure.

glossary.definition: A method used to find a solution to the problem, based on the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: To allow an intelligent computer system to play the game, we need to implement a search algorithm.

glossary.definition: Data required to solve the problem, which must be acquired by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Problem data are not given but must be acquired by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A process where a doctor develops a hypothesis about a patient's diagnosis based on the patient's symptoms and diagnostic tests.
glossary.sentence: In medical diagnosis, after the patient describes his/her symptoms, the doctor develops a hypothesis about the potential diagnosis. Taking into account that there is a variety of diagnostic tests that can be applied, the doctor orders only those that are necessary to confirm or deny a hypothesis about the patient's diagnosis (Figure 13).

glossary.definition: A rule that matches the data of the problem and produces an increasing number of conclusions or goals.
glossary.sentence: There are a large number of rules that match the data of the problem and thus produce an increasing number of conclusions or goals.

glossary.definition: A strategy that eliminates most branches by early selection of a goal, reducing the number of paths to explore.
glossary.sentence: Early selection of a goal can eliminate most of these branches. The state space graph that you see in Figure 14 demonstrates this case because it shows that there are many goals and many paths leading to them.

glossary.definition: The availability of data about the problem that can influence the search direction.
glossary.sentence: To select a search direction, it is necessary to analyse a problem by taking the following factors into account: availability of data about the problem; easiness of defining a search goal; the structure of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A sequence of nodes in the state space graph explored by the search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The search algorithm will first generate the path A->B->F->J. It will determine that this path does not lead to the search goal node, and the search algorithm will continue expanding nodes and will generate the path A->B->G->K.

glossary.definition: A node that is the target of the search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The node K, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is the search goal node. Therefore, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: A search strategy that backtracks to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants/ancestors and tries another path, used to prevent getting stuck in cycles or repeatedly visiting nodes.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking gives answers to the questions about the repeatedly discovered nodes and cycles. The principle of selecting the next current node is determined by a specific search algorithm (Figure 16). Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: A list used to support backtracking by keeping track of nodes that need to be expanded.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking introduces several practical aspects related to the implementation of other search algorithms (adopted from (Luger, 2009)) : a list of nodes awaiting expansion is needed to support backtracking.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that use only the state space graph or its nodes and arcs, exploring the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: In general, there are two main categories of search algorithms (Figure 1): Uninformed search algorithms use only the state space graph or, in other words, its nodes and arcs. This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform (Figure 2).
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are implemented using the same OPEN and CLOSED lists that we will continue to represent as a table (Figure 3). It is important to remember the following: both of these lists contain nodes together with their heuristic measures; in the OPEN list, all nodes must be ordered, taking into account their heuristic measures, from best to worst.

glossary.definition: A network of roads connecting settlements, used to demonstrate the difference between search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that processes only nodes and arcs of the state space graph, without additional information about the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: Uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that can distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph, using additional information about the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph. Therefore, each path differs from all of the others.

glossary.definition: A way of organizing and storing data that allows for efficient access and modification.
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are implemented using two lists. An OPEN list : it contains open nodes or, in other words, nodes that have been already generated but whose ancestors/descendants (depending on the search direction) have not been generated yet.

glossary.definition: a data structure called a priority queue, where the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list. Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: The node that initiates the search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A.

glossary.definition: data-driven search (forward chaining)
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky, 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found.

glossary.definition: a search strategy that starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011), in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: Nodes that are generated from a parent node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: Nodes that are generated from a parent node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: A step in the search algorithm where nodes are generated, expanded, and checked.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 0: The search start node is added to the OPEN list, and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: The node that is being expanded at the moment in the search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The node K, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: A node that has been already expanded and is no longer considered in the search process.
glossary.sentence: The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node.

glossary.definition: A node that has been generated but not yet expanded in the search process.
glossary.sentence: The node D, which is located on the first position of the OPEN list, becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: The process of generating descendants or ancestors from a current node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: A data structure that follows the principle of
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack.

glossary.definition: A principle where the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that restricts the depth of the search tree to a specified limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: A limit on the depth of the search tree in Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. However, one of the main problems of Depth-Limited Search is a lack of guidelines on how to choose the depth limit for a particular problem.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.

glossary.definition: A data structure that follows the 'First In, First Out' principle.
glossary.sentence: A queue works according to the 'First in, First out' principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed.

glossary.definition: The process of generating descendants or ancestors from a node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants - nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: A modification of Breadth-First Search that uses two search trees from the start and goal nodes.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search is a modification of Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: The amount of computing resources (time, memory) required for an algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources.

glossary.definition: Estimates of the time and memory required for an algorithm to process a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Russell and Norvig (2010) have provided time and memory estimates for Breadth-First Search processing a state space graph with an average of 10 descendants per node.

glossary.definition: The process of creating descendants or ancestors from a node.
glossary.sentence: It was assumed that in one second, the algorithm is able to generate 1 million nodes.

glossary.definition: Kilobyte, a unit of data storage.
glossary.sentence: It was assumed that in one second, the algorithm is able to generate 1 million nodes and each node contains 1 KB of data.

glossary.definition: Exploring the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.

glossary.definition: The path with the fewest number of steps between two nodes.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search is also guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: knowledge related to routine (daily) situations or used for solving complex problems with incomplete information when it is not possible to describe or evaluate the problem in full detail.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is applied in many areas of computer science, including game development, theorem proving, medical diagnosis systems, computer vision systems, financial systems, bioinformatics, and antivirus software.

glossary.definition: a function used to evaluate the quality or desirability of a move or state in a game, based on heuristics and not on a complete analysis of all possible moves.
glossary.sentence: Both strategies described above use a heuristic evaluation function. In further text, let’s consider guidelines for developing a heuristic evaluation function for a game.

glossary.definition: the values or scores assigned to terminal nodes in a game tree based on a heuristic evaluation function, used to guide the search for the best move.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth. It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes. Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: a function representing the heuristic evaluation function, where n is a node under consideration.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node.

glossary.definition: the distance between the search start node and node n, allowing the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available.

glossary.definition: a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: factors characterizing states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009)).

glossary.definition: a game with 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares, where players are allowed to move a tile onto an empty square with the aim of achieving the goal state.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares. Players are allowed to move a tile onto an empty square with the aim of achieving the goal state that you can see in Figure 5. We will consider only three states generated from the initial state of the game.

glossary.term: distance of tiles from their intended places
glossary.definition: a factor that can be used to evaluate nodes by summing up distances of tiles from their intended places.
glossary.sentence: Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: The number of pairs of tiles that need to be changed places to correct the order on the board.
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8). This means that we need to change the places of two tiles located close one to another, and usually, it takes more than two moves to put them back.

glossary.term: number of tiles out of place
glossary.definition: The number of tiles that are not in their correct positions.
glossary.sentence: We have found three different factors for the same game (Figure 9). We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, for example, f(n) = the number of tiles out of place f(n) = the sum of distances f(n) = the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: The total sum of the distances from each tile to its correct position.
glossary.sentence: We have found three different factors for the same game (Figure 9). We can use each of them alone as a heuristic evaluation function, for example, f(n) = the number of tiles out of place f(n) = the sum of distances f(n) = the number of tile reversals.

glossary.definition: The number of possible moves that can be made from the current state.
glossary.sentence: For example, for the game of chess, it could use the sum of such factors as the number of different pieces, the structure of the pawns, the number of available moves, the activity of the king, etc.

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs per node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: However, sometimes we do not know precisely the number of nodes in the graph and the depth of the graph. In this case, we need to analyze the problem to estimate the approximate average number of arcs per node and the length of the longest path.

glossary.term: depth of the state space graph
glossary.definition: The length of the longest path in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: However, sometimes we do not know precisely the number of nodes in the graph and the depth of the graph. In this case, we need to analyze the problem to estimate the approximate average number of arcs per node and the length of the longest path.

glossary.definition: A state space graph that is not possible to store and process due to limited computational resources.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources. As a result, during the operation of an intelligent computer system, the state space graph is generated to the specified depth that is allowed by available computational resources.

glossary.definition: A state space graph that can be stored and processed due to sufficient computational resources.
glossary.sentence: Computational resources are enough to store and process such a graph. For example, in planning problems that must be solved before a robot starts acting in some environment, an action plan is drawn up and represented as a state space graph.

glossary.definition: a problem in game theory and computer science where a heuristic evaluation function applied with limited look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, leading to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12).
glossary.sentence: At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.

glossary.definition: At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.
glossary.sentence: This allows the reduction of the number of states in the game tree from 362,880 (if all nodes are represented) to 12*7! = 60,480 nodes.

glossary.definition: However, transformation is not always possible.
glossary.sentence: However, transformation is not always possible. As a result, to reduce the complexity of the state space graph during the operation of the intelligent computer system, you can equip the system with a more informed heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: a search algorithm that always chooses a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, or less if the best heuristic measure is defined as the smallest measure.
glossary.sentence: Thus, if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, then Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: a graph representing the current state of the game, showing the best state that can be reached after making n moves from the current state.

glossary.definition: the result of assigning heuristic measures to nodes in a complete state space graph, indicating the outcome of the game if both players play rationally, without making mistakes, and with the aim of winning the game.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result. It clearly shows the result of the game if both players play rationally, without making mistakes, and with the aim of winning the game.

glossary.definition: a search algorithm used in game theory and computer science to determine the best possible move for a player, based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth. glossary.definition: a search strategy used in game theory and computer science that explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner, going one level deeper at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game. In this situation, it is not possible to use N-ply look-ahead, since a sub-graph may be so huge that the time limit could expire before it can be analysed. Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009). It starts with depth limit of 1, and evaluates nodes at this depth using a heuristic evaluation function. Acquired heuristic measures are propagated up to the current node using the rules of the minimax algorithm or alpha-beta pruning. The algorithm then checks whether there is sufficient time for further analysis. If so, it explores the sub-graph to a depth limit of 2 and carries out an evaluation of the nodes and propagates the heuristic measures. Then, if the time limit still has not been reached, it continues to depth limits of 3, 4, etc. In this case, it is always possible to abort the current search at any time and to find the best move based on the results acquired in the previous iteration (Rich et al., 2009).

glossary.definition: a cell in the brain that processes and transmits information, consisting of a cell body (soma), dendrites, and an axon.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons. The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron. A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron. When the signal from the sending neuron reaches the synapse, it generates an electrical signal in the dendrites of the receiving neuron. Thus, the primary activity of a neuron is to fire an electrical impulse along its axon. The single most important fact about the firing of neurons is that it depends upon the activity at the synapses. Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron. If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)).

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert.

glossary.definition: A person who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain. The domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky, 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011). Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data. Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations. Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms. Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints. Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints. Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains). Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data. Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations. Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms. Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints. Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints. Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains). Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: Means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created. Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/concept; procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created. Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/concept; procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc. All the mentioned concepts define the meaning of the concept “university”. Research works in psychology have revealed that concepts are not only related by associations but they are also organized in a hierarchical manner.

glossary.definition: Organizing knowledge in a hierarchical manner. In 1969, Collins and Quillian made an experiment with aim of understanding how people store knowledge. They asked people about birds and their features and recorded reaction time. The mean reaction time changed in relation to the complexity of a sentence. This proved that knowledge is stored at different hierarchical levels and time is needed to go along the path from the activated concept to its features. The results of the experiment are displayed in Figure 3.
glossary.sentence: The results of the experiment are displayed in Figure 3. So, the previously described experiment showed that the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird fly?” is higher than the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird sing?” This is because people do not store all features of a specific bird, but they organize knowledge about birds in a hierarchical manner: general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept “Bird” and, as a result, to find an answer people need to move along the hierarchical path in the structure of the memory.

glossary.definition: A data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation. It is a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept or, in other words, information relevant to stereotyped entities.
glossary.sentence: A frame is 'a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation' (Minsky, 1975). In other words, it is a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept (Callan, 2003; Negnevitsky, 2011) or, in other words, information relevant to stereotyped entities (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A property of objects/concepts. Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky, 2011; Winston, 1992). Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: A value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: An essential feature of frame-based expert systems. The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems (Negnevitsky, 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11).

glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation. It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole . In different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases, this type of relationship can be represented by slot titles 'a-part-of' or 'part-whole'.

A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system (Negnevitsky, 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: allowing adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem
glossary.sentence: A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: Conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order. Such an approach determines the conditions for successful problem solving.
glossary.sentence: It is well known that problem domain experts use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order. Such an approach determines the conditions for successful problem solving.

glossary.definition: rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009)
glossary.sentence: The working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011)
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009)
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009)
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern.

glossary.definition: separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001)
glossary.sentence: The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs, may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules (Negnevitsky, 2011)
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs, may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle 'Recognize-act' makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: searches for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle 'Recognize-act' makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: the selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution.

glossary.definition: is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred (Negnevitsky, 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred (Negnevitsky, 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it (Negnevitsky, 2011). In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky, 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining, an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it (Negnevitsky, 2011). In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky, 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.

glossary.definition: A type of inference engine where a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: A type of inference engine where a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”.

glossary.definition: There are different strategies how to resolve conflicts in production systems, for example: to fire the first/the last rule found.
glossary.sentence: There are different strategies how to resolve conflicts in production systems, for example: to fire the first/the last rule found.

glossary.definition: Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen.
glossary.sentence: Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen.

glossary.definition: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.

glossary.definition: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.
glossary.sentence: A rule is more specific than another if it has more conditions: IF a THEN c IF a AND b THEN c more specific rule fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory.

glossary.definition: Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented.
glossary.sentence: Uniform structure. Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented.

glossary.definition: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules.
glossary.sentence: Separation of knowledge and control. Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules.

glossary.definition: Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.

glossary.definition: The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules. At each stage of the control cycle

glossary.definition: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.
glossary.sentence: Dealing with incomplete and uncertain knowledge. Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.
